title: You don't have to use dating apps. But if you do - some tips!
intro: If you use dating apps, you'll know that they often suck. They're not the only way to find a romantic connection, but if you want to use them, we have tips. Here's one: When you match with someone, try to hop on a video call as soon as possible. You can think of it as a pre-interview.Follow us on Instagram: @nprlifekitSign up for our newsletter here.Have an episode idea or feedback you want to share? Email us at lifekit@npr.orgSupport the show and listen to it sponsor-free by signing up for Life Kit+ at plus.npr.org/lifekit

transcript.author: MARIELLE SEGARRA, HOST
transcript.text: You're listening to LIFE KIT...

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: ...From NPR.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Hey. It's Marielle. I want you to raise your hand if you've been personally victimized by dating apps. Yeah, that's a "Mean Girls" reference. I am a millennial. Sue me. But seriously, why is it that so many people hate dating apps - absolutely hate them - but still feel like they have to be on them?

transcript.author: MARISA COHEN
transcript.text: I recently just did a talk in New York, and a lot of people - it was on relationship science and the factors of interpersonal attraction.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: This is relationship scientist and marriage and family therapist Marisa Cohen.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: I had a line of people that came up to me at the end of the talk, and they were saying, feel like I have to be on the apps. Feels like everyone is meeting that way or there's the expectation that I can only find my partner using this approach.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Now, we've done a whole episode on how to date outside of the apps, and I'm a big proponent of that, but a lot of people still want to try them. And as Marisa puts it, they can be an access point - a way to meet people that you wouldn't otherwise.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: There's going to be some that you click with, some that you don't, and some that you certainly just, like, do not want to have ever crossed paths with.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: But it seems like the key is keeping the apps in perspective. They are not the only way you can meet people. They are not a second job you're supposed to take on. You can get addicted to them, and you may be using them for the wrong reasons. You think and say you're looking for a romantic connection, but actually you're looking for validation, or you got sucked into the game.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: On this episode of LIFE KIT, I talk to Marisa about how to have a healthier relationship with dating apps if you are going to use them. We'll talk about how to set boundaries for yourself around the apps, how to be clear about what you want and waste as little time as possible in the dating app messaging vortex. And we'll also go through the signs that your app use is becoming unhealthy. That's coming up after the break.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: When I was using the apps, I noticed that I felt drawn in by them in a similar way that I feel drawn in by my phone in general - that they can have an addictive quality. And I would often find myself swiping mindlessly and also just feeling bad after every time I opened the app.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: A hundred percent. I mean, dating apps, just like with any other app, it's gamified. You know, you'll get notifications. Maybe, depending on what app you're using, someone sent you a like, someone sent you a rose, and it's a little dopamine hit every time you get that notification, so it keeps you in it. And there's intermittent reinforcement, which is basically the same principles that slot machines operate under. So we're always waiting for that hit, that like, that match, and it certainly can keep us in this cycle of always being on your phone, always staying connected.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: And then beyond that, a lot of people approach dating apps almost with this fear of, what if I'm not active and the person that I click with or the person that I'm "supposed to meet" - and I'm putting that in air quotes - is on the app and active at that time and I'm not there to talk to them? So it almost keeps people in this place of needing to constantly monitor who's new on the app, who's sending messages, and it can really just take a lot of time and energy.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: I know a lot of people will fill up their social calendar completely with dates, and then this ends up feeling like a second job.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: Exactly. I actually had a friend who, during grad school, you know, her mantra was, it's a numbers game, it's a numbers game. And she used to set it up that she wanted to meet every single potential partner in her dating pool. And she would go for a lunch date and dinner date six out of seven days of the week and was just like, let me cycle through as many of my matches as quickly as possible. And she really just wound up getting burnt out pretty quickly.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: What are some ways people can set boundaries around the apps?

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: So I think that, you know, it depends upon the person and what feels comfortable for them. You know, some people, it might look like engaging with the apps just for a limited amount of time per day or only certain days of the week, or maybe just opening up their profiles one time per week or a couple of times per month. Again, like, you really have to get a sense of, am I experiencing burnout? Am I starting to approach these dates in a frustrated way that won't lead to a good outcome? And then based upon that, that's how you set your boundary. For some people, it just helps them to take a break entirely for a specified period of time, which is until they feel ready to approach dating again. That's perfectly fine, as well.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: I wonder if it can help to say - and this might be a little hard to track, but if you said to yourself, like, all right, if I'm going to use the dating apps, I'm only allowed to swipe on - I don't know - 20 people today.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: That can be helpful 'cause a lot of people just kind of like - it's like it almost becomes a fun game. And people are...

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Yeah.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: ...Kind of quickly going through the profiles, often just looking at the photo and maybe just, like, a tagline, and they aren't reading the actual bios. So if you swipe too quickly, you're potentially missing out on some incredible people that, had you otherwise met them, let's say, like, through friends or started a conversation with them in the real world, you might have actually been interested in them. Because there's that whole idea of, there's an infinite number of people in my dating pool, so I can just, like, quickly go through hundreds of profiles. Especially if you're in, like, a large urban area like New York City or Boston or Philadelphia, it's, like, there's so many people on the app that you can just - it can become a game.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Takeaway one - set boundaries with yourself around your app use. Dating apps are gamified to keep you coming back. Make sure you're using them, not letting them use you. Consider time blocking. For instance, you could say, I'm only going to open these apps at a designated time for half an hour and then block them the rest of the time. Or you could try limiting your number of swipes, like, I'm only going to swipe on 15 people tonight, or whatever number you choose. After that, I'm closing the app. The idea here is to embrace a slower pace to dating and to not let the fear of missing out drive your behavior.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: It seems like it's a good idea if you're using dating apps to also be really honest with yourself about what you're using them for.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: Absolutely. I think that, you know, some people are just scrolling through them, just kind of surveying, like, who is potentially out there for me. But, like, what's the end goal here? Are you looking for a relationship, and what type of relationship are you looking for? And it's also helpful to make sure that your profile matches your true intentions. And that's the first thing I tell people is - you know, a lot of people try to optimize their profile so they're going to get presented to as many people as possible and they try to, like, figure out, well, what is it in my profile that will stand out to others? What might they want? But if that doesn't accurately represent who you are as a person, that match isn't going to work out for you in the long run anyway.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Yeah. You're just making the whole experience more chaotic for yourself and for them.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: Exactly. And that's why people get frustrated - because they see these profiles, they maybe pick up on one aspect of them, start to engage in conversation, and everything seems great, great, great. But then, all of a sudden, when you meet in person, you find out that this individual doesn't share the same interests or, you know, maybe hyped up an aspect of their life that you were really attracted to, and then that's not really a big part of their life. So represent yourself as you are because that's how you're going to attract the kind of people who appreciate you.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Takeaway two - be clear with yourself about why you're using the apps. Ask yourself, in an ideal scenario, what are you hoping to find on the apps - love, sex, friendship, validation, excitement? And what are you actually finding? And then be clear with other people in your profile and in your conversations.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: What are some examples of ways to be more direct in your profile?

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: Look; a profile is difficult because it's static and human beings are dynamic, of course. So it's really hard to showcase your true personality in a way that's going to really, like, allow yourself to shine through on a little bio. But I tell people to make sure that - like, most people spend time on photos - the most time on photos. And that's what they're making a lot of their decisions on, unfortunately. But showcase in your photos different aspects of your life that are important to you. If you are someone who doesn't like to travel but you perceive that - you know, people want to hear that you're a world traveler, so you put as your main photo that one time that you went on vacation, that's not a great representation of you. So showcase - if you're an animal lover, it should be a picture with, like, your dog or cat or whatever pet you have. If, you know, you like to volunteer, maybe a photo volunteering. Like, display the aspects of your life that are important, and then in your bio, just also make sure that it matches and you reflect that.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: One thing I always hated was messaging with strangers and making small talk for who knows how long. So I started writing in my profile, I don't like to be on my phone a lot. So if we match, let's set up a quick video call to feel out the vibe and see if we want to meet in person.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: I love that. I tell people that they should get to the first date as quickly as they can that they feel comfortable with. That's kind of like that first almost screener of, are we able to hold a conversation rather than just become pen pals? I think that, you know, going on these long, protracted conversations through the app or even texting, that's not really helping you learn who the person is. You really have to assess that compatibility face-to-face.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Yeah. It's so awkward when you meet somebody who you've been texting with, and then you're like, you don't really look like your picture, or you just don't - your vibe is weird or I don't like the way you smell.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: Right, right. Well, I mean, you know, you kind of, like, develop this back-and-forth rapport with a person, and people sometimes have texting behaviors that match their personality. Sometimes they don't. But you really need to see, is this someone that I enjoy engaging with and can see myself continuing to date them? And you're not going to really be able to suss that out through texting alone.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Takeaway three - save yourself time and energy by cutting down on the messaging and getting to a date as quickly as possible. You can signal that you want to do this in your profile. Also consider setting up a quick 10-minute video call with prospective dates. If they drag their feet or they keep rescheduling, move on. This method can save you a lot of trouble.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: By the way, none of what I'm saying here is a magic hack that's going to finally break the apps open for you and lead you to love. But these tips will help you protect your sanity while you use the apps as a tool and decide if you want to keep using them. We'll have more LIFE KIT after the break.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: I think the maybe elephant in the room with dating apps, for me, was always sex. It felt like you go on a date with someone who's a stranger, and there's this neon sign blaring over your head like, are you going to have sex with them, and when? I felt like that made it a lot harder to be on the apps because it was almost like, oh, people have these expectations.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: There are certainly people who are on the apps looking to hook up, looking to have sex. There are apps that are just geared towards that, which, great, but be clear about what it is that you're looking for. Now, as far as, you know, a person who may be looking for a relationship and now feels like there's a pressure of sex, of course, it's so important to move at a pace that feels comfortable for you. I've had a lot of clients who have said, oh, the three-date rule, the three-date rule. And this is for people that they've just met organically out there in real life or through friends. And that's kind of like that, you know, by the third date, if you want to assess if it's a match, there's got to be some sort of physical intimacy at that point. And that doesn't work for everyone, and you need to do - like, you need to move at a pace that is comfortable and safe for you.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Yeah. And I think, like, try to have confidence in your own boundaries.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: Which is a challenge. It's a challenge, but it's first taking a moment to reflect and think about what feels safe, what feels healthy for me. And then based upon that, what are the important boundaries that I need to set? And then it's setting and maintaining those boundaries. And that's going to lead towards finding a healthy connection with another person.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: The other thing I was thinking about in terms of boundaries or knowing what you're using the apps for, if you know that you don't want to be polyamorous, for instance, like, where you would meet someone on the apps, and then they'd be like, well, I - you know, I also have, like, another partner, and then they have another partner. If you know that that's not for you, I feel like it's really important to not compromise on that 'cause sometimes you'd be like, oh, but it's been a while. I haven't been on any good dates in a while, and here's this person. They're so cute. Maybe I'll just go out with them. But if it's really not for you, then you're just hurting yourself.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: Your needs are important. Your needs are important. Your wants are important. If you're looking for a monogamous relationship, you are looking for a monogamous relationship, end stop. Like that. And that is fine.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Yeah.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: If you are looking for a polyamorous relationship, that is what you are looking for. Even if you meet a really great person who doesn't have the same relationship-related goals or lifestyle or orientation or whatever it is, they're not going to be a match. They may be a great friend, but they're not going to be a match for you. And as frustrating as that might be, that's OK, and you need to wait for the person that you can enter into a healthy, loving, caring relationship with.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: I think about this idea that comes from the negotiation world, the BATNA, the best alternative to a negotiated agreement. And a lot of the time, if you're negotiating over salary, unless you have another offer, your employer may assume, like, you're not going to walk away from this, and they may be right. But, technically, in romance, you don't need a partner. And if you understand that or you really feel that, then you have a really good BATNA, right? Like, your best alternative to dating this person who's not a good fit for you is being solo, and that's cool. Like, that's great.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: Yes, I love the way that you said that. It is much better to be happily single than to be in a relationship that isn't meeting your needs because it won't do anything for your overall well-being or life satisfaction, and as tempting as it could be, especially, you know, with dating apps where there's this perception and maybe reality that there are so many people out there. If you're constantly dipping into the pool to see, like, what are my alternatives, you're one foot out of the relationship, and you're not necessarily giving your attention and focus to the relationship that you're in.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Takeaway four - don't compromise on the things that are most important to you. And remember that not being in a relationship or being romantically involved with anyone is a perfectly good option.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Big picture - we've talked a lot about mindset here and the kind of headspace you should be in when you're using the apps. What are some warning signs that your relationship with dating apps is becoming unhealthy?

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: Like with any behavioral addiction, I'm talking specifically about app usage right now when there's a loss of awareness of time, where you go on the app, you're thinking, I'm just going to swipe through a couple of profiles, and then all of a sudden you've missed dinner or it went from being light outside to dark outside and you're like, where did all this time go? Maybe your usage is not the most helpful. I think that if you start basically, like, convincing yourself of, you know, the - well, I should be doing this. Once the should statements like, I should be going on dates. I should be active on the app. There are no such thing as shoulds in this case. Like, you need to do what's right for you. So if you're now comparing yourself to what you believe, like, ideal dating app behavior or ideal dating behavior is or what your friends are doing, it's time to reexamine your usage.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: And, look; dating anxiety is a real thing, so people may get very excited about the match and then start to feel a little bit nervous when the date approaches. However, if you're finding, like, I can't - like, I don't want to do this, or I don't - like, it seems like such a wasted night to have to - when you start dreading the date, if you set the date for, like, a week in advance and then as a date approaches, you're just like, I don't want to do this, even if the person is great, you're not going to recognize it as such. They might pleasantly surprise you. That might change your mood. But you're not giving them the best version of you, so it's going to be a waste of both of your time. So that might be time to reexamine as well. That's also times when people are more likely to settle, and they're just like, person's good enough. Like, it's just - it's an item that I have to take off of my to-do list, and that may cause a person to forego some of the things that they were really looking for in a partner because it's truly important to them, and that relationship is not going to be set up for success.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Takeaway five - pay attention to how you use the apps. If you notice that you're getting sucked in, like you're just swiping, swiping, swiping and then all of a sudden you miss dinner or an important meeting, that's a sign to pull back. If you're only on the apps because you think, oh, I should be doing this, that's a sign. If you find yourself dreading all the dates you set up, that's a sign.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Yeah, sometimes people are like, I'm tired of the apps, so you'll do.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: Right.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: But you can say, I'm tired of the apps. I'm going to stop using them.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: Right.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Full stop.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: Right. And it's not even just the apps. It could be dating. It can be, I'm tired of dating right now. I'm going to redevote my attention and energy to my friendships, to these activities that I really enjoy doing that make me feel fulfilled. You don't need to be on the hunt for a partner if that's not feeling like something that's true for you in that moment.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Marisa, thank you so much. This is a great conversation.

transcript.author: COHEN
transcript.text: Oh, thank you. I really appreciate it.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: All right, seekers of romance, it's time for a recap. Takeaway one - set boundaries with yourself around your app use. Dating apps are gamified to keep you coming back, so make sure you're using them, not letting them use you.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Takeaway two - be clear with yourself about why you're using the apps. Are you seeking love, sex, friendship, validation, excitement? And what are you actually finding? Then be clear with other people in your profile and in your conversations.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Takeaway three - save yourself time and energy by cutting down on the messaging and getting to a date as quickly as possible. You can say that you want to do this in your profile. Also consider setting up a quick 10-minute video call with prospective dates. That'll save you a lot of time, too.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Takeaway four - don't compromise on the things that are most important to you, and remember that not being in a relationship or being romantically involved is a perfectly good option.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: And takeaway five - pay attention to the way you use dating apps and if they're negatively affecting your mental health, and be willing to step away.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: The last thing I want to say here is when I was on the apps, I started to resent the idea that something as personal as romantic love had to be mediated through this device, my smartphone. I said, you know what? I've had enough. I'm not finding what I'm seeking here. So let me stop seeking it for a while and just live. It was a moment of surrender. And I have learned that you absolutely can find romantic connection off the apps. But when I stopped using them, that wasn't even the point. The point was simply I didn't want to do it anymore. And once I made that decision, I felt so relieved. This option is available to you, too.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: All right. That's our show. By the way, have you subscribed to the LIFE KIT newsletter yet? Every Friday, you'll get even more expert advice on health, money and more, all curated by the LIFE KIT team. Think of it as a weekly check-in from your friends at LIFE KIT. Subscribe at npr.org/lifekitnewsletter.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: This episode of LIFE KIT was produced by Mika Ellison and Margaret Cirino. Our digital editor is Malaka Gharib. Our visuals editor is CJ Riculan. Meghan Keane is our senior supervising editor, and Beth Donovan is our executive producer. Our production team also includes Andee Tagle, Clare Marie Schneider and Sylvie Douglis. Engineering support comes from Jimmy Keeley. I'm Marielle Segarra. Thanks for listening.

lines: You're listening to LIFE KIT...
...From NPR.

Hey. It's Marielle. I want you to raise your hand if you've been personally victimized by dating apps. Yeah, that's a "Mean Girls" reference. I am a millennial. Sue me. But seriously, why is it that so many people hate dating apps - absolutely hate them - but still feel like they have to be on them?
I recently just did a talk in New York, and a lot of people - it was on relationship science and the factors of interpersonal attraction.
This is relationship scientist and marriage and family therapist Marisa Cohen.

I had a line of people that came up to me at the end of the talk, and they were saying, feel like I have to be on the apps. Feels like everyone is meeting that way or there's the expectation that I can only find my partner using this approach.

Now, we've done a whole episode on how to date outside of the apps, and I'm a big proponent of that, but a lot of people still want to try them. And as Marisa puts it, they can be an access point - a way to meet people that you wouldn't otherwise.
There's going to be some that you click with, some that you don't, and some that you certainly just, like, do not want to have ever crossed paths with.

But it seems like the key is keeping the apps in perspective. They are not the only way you can meet people. They are not a second job you're supposed to take on. You can get addicted to them, and you may be using them for the wrong reasons. You think and say you're looking for a romantic connection, but actually you're looking for validation, or you got sucked into the game.

On this episode of LIFE KIT, I talk to Marisa about how to have a healthier relationship with dating apps if you are going to use them. We'll talk about how to set boundaries for yourself around the apps, how to be clear about what you want and waste as little time as possible in the dating app messaging vortex. And we'll also go through the signs that your app use is becoming unhealthy. That's coming up after the break.

When I was using the apps, I noticed that I felt drawn in by them in a similar way that I feel drawn in by my phone in general - that they can have an addictive quality. And I would often find myself swiping mindlessly and also just feeling bad after every time I opened the app.

A hundred percent. I mean, dating apps, just like with any other app, it's gamified. You know, you'll get notifications. Maybe, depending on what app you're using, someone sent you a like, someone sent you a rose, and it's a little dopamine hit every time you get that notification, so it keeps you in it. And there's intermittent reinforcement, which is basically the same principles that slot machines operate under. So we're always waiting for that hit, that like, that match, and it certainly can keep us in this cycle of always being on your phone, always staying connected.

And then beyond that, a lot of people approach dating apps almost with this fear of, what if I'm not active and the person that I click with or the person that I'm "supposed to meet" - and I'm putting that in air quotes - is on the app and active at that time and I'm not there to talk to them? So it almost keeps people in this place of needing to constantly monitor who's new on the app, who's sending messages, and it can really just take a lot of time and energy.
I know a lot of people will fill up their social calendar completely with dates, and then this ends up feeling like a second job.

Exactly. I actually had a friend who, during grad school, you know, her mantra was, it's a numbers game, it's a numbers game. And she used to set it up that she wanted to meet every single potential partner in her dating pool. And she would go for a lunch date and dinner date six out of seven days of the week and was just like, let me cycle through as many of my matches as quickly as possible. And she really just wound up getting burnt out pretty quickly.
What are some ways people can set boundaries around the apps?

So I think that, you know, it depends upon the person and what feels comfortable for them. You know, some people, it might look like engaging with the apps just for a limited amount of time per day or only certain days of the week, or maybe just opening up their profiles one time per week or a couple of times per month. Again, like, you really have to get a sense of, am I experiencing burnout? Am I starting to approach these dates in a frustrated way that won't lead to a good outcome? And then based upon that, that's how you set your boundary. For some people, it just helps them to take a break entirely for a specified period of time, which is until they feel ready to approach dating again. That's perfectly fine, as well.

I wonder if it can help to say - and this might be a little hard to track, but if you said to yourself, like, all right, if I'm going to use the dating apps, I'm only allowed to swipe on - I don't know - 20 people today.
That can be helpful 'cause a lot of people just kind of like - it's like it almost becomes a fun game. And people are...
Yeah.

...Kind of quickly going through the profiles, often just looking at the photo and maybe just, like, a tagline, and they aren't reading the actual bios. So if you swipe too quickly, you're potentially missing out on some incredible people that, had you otherwise met them, let's say, like, through friends or started a conversation with them in the real world, you might have actually been interested in them. Because there's that whole idea of, there's an infinite number of people in my dating pool, so I can just, like, quickly go through hundreds of profiles. Especially if you're in, like, a large urban area like New York City or Boston or Philadelphia, it's, like, there's so many people on the app that you can just - it can become a game.

Takeaway one - set boundaries with yourself around your app use. Dating apps are gamified to keep you coming back. Make sure you're using them, not letting them use you. Consider time blocking. For instance, you could say, I'm only going to open these apps at a designated time for half an hour and then block them the rest of the time. Or you could try limiting your number of swipes, like, I'm only going to swipe on 15 people tonight, or whatever number you choose. After that, I'm closing the app. The idea here is to embrace a slower pace to dating and to not let the fear of missing out drive your behavior.
It seems like it's a good idea if you're using dating apps to also be really honest with yourself about what you're using them for.

Absolutely. I think that, you know, some people are just scrolling through them, just kind of surveying, like, who is potentially out there for me. But, like, what's the end goal here? Are you looking for a relationship, and what type of relationship are you looking for? And it's also helpful to make sure that your profile matches your true intentions. And that's the first thing I tell people is - you know, a lot of people try to optimize their profile so they're going to get presented to as many people as possible and they try to, like, figure out, well, what is it in my profile that will stand out to others? What might they want? But if that doesn't accurately represent who you are as a person, that match isn't going to work out for you in the long run anyway.
Yeah. You're just making the whole experience more chaotic for yourself and for them.

Exactly. And that's why people get frustrated - because they see these profiles, they maybe pick up on one aspect of them, start to engage in conversation, and everything seems great, great, great. But then, all of a sudden, when you meet in person, you find out that this individual doesn't share the same interests or, you know, maybe hyped up an aspect of their life that you were really attracted to, and then that's not really a big part of their life. So represent yourself as you are because that's how you're going to attract the kind of people who appreciate you.

Takeaway two - be clear with yourself about why you're using the apps. Ask yourself, in an ideal scenario, what are you hoping to find on the apps - love, sex, friendship, validation, excitement? And what are you actually finding? And then be clear with other people in your profile and in your conversations.
What are some examples of ways to be more direct in your profile?

Look; a profile is difficult because it's static and human beings are dynamic, of course. So it's really hard to showcase your true personality in a way that's going to really, like, allow yourself to shine through on a little bio. But I tell people to make sure that - like, most people spend time on photos - the most time on photos. And that's what they're making a lot of their decisions on, unfortunately. But showcase in your photos different aspects of your life that are important to you. If you are someone who doesn't like to travel but you perceive that - you know, people want to hear that you're a world traveler, so you put as your main photo that one time that you went on vacation, that's not a great representation of you. So showcase - if you're an animal lover, it should be a picture with, like, your dog or cat or whatever pet you have. If, you know, you like to volunteer, maybe a photo volunteering. Like, display the aspects of your life that are important, and then in your bio, just also make sure that it matches and you reflect that.

One thing I always hated was messaging with strangers and making small talk for who knows how long. So I started writing in my profile, I don't like to be on my phone a lot. So if we match, let's set up a quick video call to feel out the vibe and see if we want to meet in person.

I love that. I tell people that they should get to the first date as quickly as they can that they feel comfortable with. That's kind of like that first almost screener of, are we able to hold a conversation rather than just become pen pals? I think that, you know, going on these long, protracted conversations through the app or even texting, that's not really helping you learn who the person is. You really have to assess that compatibility face-to-face.

Yeah. It's so awkward when you meet somebody who you've been texting with, and then you're like, you don't really look like your picture, or you just don't - your vibe is weird or I don't like the way you smell.

Right, right. Well, I mean, you know, you kind of, like, develop this back-and-forth rapport with a person, and people sometimes have texting behaviors that match their personality. Sometimes they don't. But you really need to see, is this someone that I enjoy engaging with and can see myself continuing to date them? And you're not going to really be able to suss that out through texting alone.

Takeaway three - save yourself time and energy by cutting down on the messaging and getting to a date as quickly as possible. You can signal that you want to do this in your profile. Also consider setting up a quick 10-minute video call with prospective dates. If they drag their feet or they keep rescheduling, move on. This method can save you a lot of trouble.

By the way, none of what I'm saying here is a magic hack that's going to finally break the apps open for you and lead you to love. But these tips will help you protect your sanity while you use the apps as a tool and decide if you want to keep using them. We'll have more LIFE KIT after the break.

I think the maybe elephant in the room with dating apps, for me, was always sex. It felt like you go on a date with someone who's a stranger, and there's this neon sign blaring over your head like, are you going to have sex with them, and when? I felt like that made it a lot harder to be on the apps because it was almost like, oh, people have these expectations.

There are certainly people who are on the apps looking to hook up, looking to have sex. There are apps that are just geared towards that, which, great, but be clear about what it is that you're looking for. Now, as far as, you know, a person who may be looking for a relationship and now feels like there's a pressure of sex, of course, it's so important to move at a pace that feels comfortable for you. I've had a lot of clients who have said, oh, the three-date rule, the three-date rule. And this is for people that they've just met organically out there in real life or through friends. And that's kind of like that, you know, by the third date, if you want to assess if it's a match, there's got to be some sort of physical intimacy at that point. And that doesn't work for everyone, and you need to do - like, you need to move at a pace that is comfortable and safe for you.
Yeah. And I think, like, try to have confidence in your own boundaries.

Which is a challenge. It's a challenge, but it's first taking a moment to reflect and think about what feels safe, what feels healthy for me. And then based upon that, what are the important boundaries that I need to set? And then it's setting and maintaining those boundaries. And that's going to lead towards finding a healthy connection with another person.

The other thing I was thinking about in terms of boundaries or knowing what you're using the apps for, if you know that you don't want to be polyamorous, for instance, like, where you would meet someone on the apps, and then they'd be like, well, I - you know, I also have, like, another partner, and then they have another partner. If you know that that's not for you, I feel like it's really important to not compromise on that 'cause sometimes you'd be like, oh, but it's been a while. I haven't been on any good dates in a while, and here's this person. They're so cute. Maybe I'll just go out with them. But if it's really not for you, then you're just hurting yourself.

Your needs are important. Your needs are important. Your wants are important. If you're looking for a monogamous relationship, you are looking for a monogamous relationship, end stop. Like that. And that is fine.
Yeah.

If you are looking for a polyamorous relationship, that is what you are looking for. Even if you meet a really great person who doesn't have the same relationship-related goals or lifestyle or orientation or whatever it is, they're not going to be a match. They may be a great friend, but they're not going to be a match for you. And as frustrating as that might be, that's OK, and you need to wait for the person that you can enter into a healthy, loving, caring relationship with.

I think about this idea that comes from the negotiation world, the BATNA, the best alternative to a negotiated agreement. And a lot of the time, if you're negotiating over salary, unless you have another offer, your employer may assume, like, you're not going to walk away from this, and they may be right. But, technically, in romance, you don't need a partner. And if you understand that or you really feel that, then you have a really good BATNA, right? Like, your best alternative to dating this person who's not a good fit for you is being solo, and that's cool. Like, that's great.

Yes, I love the way that you said that. It is much better to be happily single than to be in a relationship that isn't meeting your needs because it won't do anything for your overall well-being or life satisfaction, and as tempting as it could be, especially, you know, with dating apps where there's this perception and maybe reality that there are so many people out there. If you're constantly dipping into the pool to see, like, what are my alternatives, you're one foot out of the relationship, and you're not necessarily giving your attention and focus to the relationship that you're in.
Takeaway four - don't compromise on the things that are most important to you. And remember that not being in a relationship or being romantically involved with anyone is a perfectly good option.

Big picture - we've talked a lot about mindset here and the kind of headspace you should be in when you're using the apps. What are some warning signs that your relationship with dating apps is becoming unhealthy?

Like with any behavioral addiction, I'm talking specifically about app usage right now when there's a loss of awareness of time, where you go on the app, you're thinking, I'm just going to swipe through a couple of profiles, and then all of a sudden you've missed dinner or it went from being light outside to dark outside and you're like, where did all this time go? Maybe your usage is not the most helpful. I think that if you start basically, like, convincing yourself of, you know, the - well, I should be doing this. Once the should statements like, I should be going on dates. I should be active on the app. There are no such thing as shoulds in this case. Like, you need to do what's right for you. So if you're now comparing yourself to what you believe, like, ideal dating app behavior or ideal dating behavior is or what your friends are doing, it's time to reexamine your usage.

And, look; dating anxiety is a real thing, so people may get very excited about the match and then start to feel a little bit nervous when the date approaches. However, if you're finding, like, I can't - like, I don't want to do this, or I don't - like, it seems like such a wasted night to have to - when you start dreading the date, if you set the date for, like, a week in advance and then as a date approaches, you're just like, I don't want to do this, even if the person is great, you're not going to recognize it as such. They might pleasantly surprise you. That might change your mood. But you're not giving them the best version of you, so it's going to be a waste of both of your time. So that might be time to reexamine as well. That's also times when people are more likely to settle, and they're just like, person's good enough. Like, it's just - it's an item that I have to take off of my to-do list, and that may cause a person to forego some of the things that they were really looking for in a partner because it's truly important to them, and that relationship is not going to be set up for success.

Takeaway five - pay attention to how you use the apps. If you notice that you're getting sucked in, like you're just swiping, swiping, swiping and then all of a sudden you miss dinner or an important meeting, that's a sign to pull back. If you're only on the apps because you think, oh, I should be doing this, that's a sign. If you find yourself dreading all the dates you set up, that's a sign.
Yeah, sometimes people are like, I'm tired of the apps, so you'll do.
Right.
But you can say, I'm tired of the apps. I'm going to stop using them.
Right.
Full stop.

Right. And it's not even just the apps. It could be dating. It can be, I'm tired of dating right now. I'm going to redevote my attention and energy to my friendships, to these activities that I really enjoy doing that make me feel fulfilled. You don't need to be on the hunt for a partner if that's not feeling like something that's true for you in that moment.
Marisa, thank you so much. This is a great conversation.
Oh, thank you. I really appreciate it.

All right, seekers of romance, it's time for a recap. Takeaway one - set boundaries with yourself around your app use. Dating apps are gamified to keep you coming back, so make sure you're using them, not letting them use you.

Takeaway two - be clear with yourself about why you're using the apps. Are you seeking love, sex, friendship, validation, excitement? And what are you actually finding? Then be clear with other people in your profile and in your conversations.

Takeaway three - save yourself time and energy by cutting down on the messaging and getting to a date as quickly as possible. You can say that you want to do this in your profile. Also consider setting up a quick 10-minute video call with prospective dates. That'll save you a lot of time, too.
Takeaway four - don't compromise on the things that are most important to you, and remember that not being in a relationship or being romantically involved is a perfectly good option.
And takeaway five - pay attention to the way you use dating apps and if they're negatively affecting your mental health, and be willing to step away.

The last thing I want to say here is when I was on the apps, I started to resent the idea that something as personal as romantic love had to be mediated through this device, my smartphone. I said, you know what? I've had enough. I'm not finding what I'm seeking here. So let me stop seeking it for a while and just live. It was a moment of surrender. And I have learned that you absolutely can find romantic connection off the apps. But when I stopped using them, that wasn't even the point. The point was simply I didn't want to do it anymore. And once I made that decision, I felt so relieved. This option is available to you, too.
